root_cause: regex-svc had its vulnerable WAF regex rule enabled. The load container was continuously sending a crafted payload to /check that triggered catastrophic backtracking, saturating the service worker pool and one CPU core so normal health checks and user requests timed out even though the process was still running.
actions_taken: Inspected running containers, logs, resource usage, service environment, and the Compose configuration. Verified regex-svc was pegged at about 100% CPU while the load generator targeted /check and the service log reported the vulnerable rule enabled. Updated only regex-svc in docker-compose.yaml to set WAF_RULE_DISABLED=1, then recreated only regex-svc with docker compose up -d regex-svc. Confirmed the recreated service logged WAF rule disabled, returned HTTP 200 quickly for the adversarial payload, and CPU usage dropped to low single digits while unrelated containers were left intact.
postmortem: A vulnerable regular-expression based WAF rule allowed a small crafted input to consume excessive CPU through catastrophic backtracking. Because regex-svc used a small fixed worker pool and a one-core CPU limit, a few slow evaluations starved health checks and customer traffic. The immediate mitigation was to disable the unsafe rule for regex-svc, matching the stable payments service configuration. Follow-up should replace the unsafe regex with a bounded implementation or RE2-style engine, add regression tests for pathological inputs, and add alerting that distinguishes process liveness from worker-pool saturation.
